source_paper_title: Liquid Welfare Guarantees for No-Regret Learning in Sequential Budgeted Auctions
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Fikioris_2025, title={Liquid Welfare Guarantees for No-Regret Learning in Sequential Budgeted Auctions}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0274}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0274}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Fikioris, Giannis and Tardos, Éva}, year={2025}, month=may, pages={1233–1249} }
context:
Page 18, Section 8 (Conclusion)

While we prove positive liquid welfare guarantees under mild learning assumptions, we leave open the problem of whether stronger learning guarantees imply better liquid welfare. For example, can we get better liquid welfare guarantees if the players have high competitive ratio with respect to the best Lipschitz continuous function that maps values to bids? Or if the values are sampled from a distribution instead of being picked adversarially?
title: Design learning guarantees that improve liquid welfare in sequential budgeted auctions
background_latex:
Consider a sequential auction with one item per round over rounds \(t\in[T]=\{1,\dots,T\}\) and players \(i\in[n]\). Player \(i\) has a hard budget \(B_i>0\) and per-round value \(v_{it}\in[0,1]\) for winning the round-\(t\) item. (In the additive-valuation model, the value of a set of won rounds \(S\subseteq[T]\) is \(\sum_{t\in S} v_{it}\).) In each round players submit bids; in a first-price auction the highest bidder wins and pays her bid. Let \(V_i\) and \(P_i\) denote player \(i\)'s total realized value and payment over all rounds, and let the (budgeted quasilinear) utility be \(U_i = V_i-P_i\) if \(P_i\le B_i\) and \(-\infty\) otherwise.

The system performance metric is liquid welfare. Player \(i\)'s liquid welfare contribution is
\[\mathrm{LW}_i := \min\{B_i, V_i\},\qquad \mathrm{LW}:=\sum_{i=1}^n \mathrm{LW}_i.\]
Let \(\mathrm{LW}^*\) denote the maximum liquid welfare over all feasible allocations of items across rounds.

Players are assumed to be learning (not necessarily converging to equilibrium). A baseline behavioral benchmark studied in the paper is comparison to the best fixed multiplicative shading multiplier \(\lambda\in[0,1]\) applied to values each round, subject to not exceeding the remaining budget. More generally, one can consider richer benchmark classes mapping values to bids; e.g., a (scalar) bidding rule \(f:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) that is Lipschitz continuous (with some Lipschitz constant \(L\)) and induces bids \(b_{it}=f(v_{it})\) each round, again subject to the remaining budget. One can also distinguish adversarially chosen value sequences \((v_{it})\) versus values drawn i.i.d. from a distribution (stochastic model).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exist stronger, still natural learning-style performance guarantees on bidders' utilities that imply strictly better worst-case approximation of optimal liquid welfare \(\mathrm{LW}^*\) in sequential budgeted first-price auctions than the bounds obtained under comparison to the best fixed shading multiplier.

In particular, analyze the liquid-welfare price of anarchy achievable when each player attains a small competitive ratio (or no-regret) with respect to richer benchmark classes such as:
1) the best Lipschitz continuous bidding function \(f:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) mapping values to bids, and/or
2) the best fixed shading multiplier when the values \((v_{it})\) are sampled from a distribution rather than chosen adversarially.

The goal is to characterize the best provable factor \(\alpha\) (as a function of the learning guarantee parameters) such that \(\mathrm{LW} \ge \mathrm{LW}^*/\alpha\) (up to lower-order additive terms, if unavoidable) under these stronger learning assumptions.

open_problem_quote: While we prove positive liquid welfare guarantees under mild learning assumptions, we leave open the problem of whether stronger learning guarantees imply better liquid welfare. For example, can we get better liquid welfare guarantees if the players have high competitive ratio with respect to the best Lipschitz continuous function that maps values to bids? Or if the values are sampled from a distribution instead of being picked adversarially?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: For any proposed improved guarantee, one can search over small numbers of players/rounds, budgets, and value sequences to try to find instances where high performance against a richer benchmark still yields poor liquid welfare. However, the benchmark involves optimizing over a function class (e.g., Lipschitz functions), which makes automated checking more complex than for a finite action set.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects (adversarial instances, value distributions, or learning guarantees/bounds) can be represented programmatically, and a fitness signal can be based on computed liquid-welfare ratios under simulated learning behavior constrained to satisfy a benchmark-competitiveness inequality. But enforcing or optimizing over Lipschitz function benchmarks and quantifying worst-case guarantees is computationally heavy and may require relaxations or proxy optimization.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Fikioris–Tardos) establishes that in sequential budgeted first-price auctions, a per-bidder learning guarantee against a very weak benchmark—best fixed multiplicative shading \(\lambda\in[0,1]\) subject to remaining budget—already implies a constant-factor approximation to optimal liquid welfare \(\mathrm{LW}^*\). Specifically, if each bidder achieves competitive ratio \(\gamma\) (plus small regret) relative to the best fixed shading multiplier, then \(\mathrm{LW}\) is within \(\gamma+1/2+O(1/\gamma)\) of \(\mathrm{LW}^*\) (additive valuations), and within \((2+\gamma+\sqrt{\gamma^2+4})/2\) for submodular valuations. Matching lower bounds show that in worst-case/adversarial instances one cannot hope for substantially better than \(\max\{\gamma,2\}\) even if bidders compete against the stronger benchmark of the best bid sequence, leaving only a narrow gap (notably between 2 and \(\approx 2.41\) when \(\gamma=1\)).

The open problem asks whether strengthening the learning benchmark class (e.g., to the best Lipschitz bidding function \(f:[0,1]\to[0,1]\)) or moving to stochastic/i.i.d. values can provably improve the worst-case liquid-welfare approximation beyond what fixed shading yields. Existing related work gives partial evidence in both directions: (i) in stochastic repeated-auction models, algorithm-specific pacing dynamics can achieve factor-2 liquid-welfare guarantees (Gaitonde et al.; Lucier et al.), suggesting that distributional structure plus particular dynamics can improve efficiency; but (ii) the source paper’s impossibility results indicate that merely strengthening the individual benchmark (even to best bid sequence) does not automatically improve worst-case liquid welfare in adversarial environments.

Methodologically, the main technical tools around this question come from budgeted online learning and Bandits with Knapsacks (BKS; Immorlica et al.; Castiglioni et al.; Fikioris–Tardos). These results enable high-probability competitive-ratio guarantees and may be adaptable to richer benchmark classes by exploiting structure (e.g., Lipschitzness) to reduce discretization error and improve regret/competitive terms. However, no existing work (in the provided set) resolves whether such stronger learning guarantees translate into strictly better liquid-welfare price-of-anarchy bounds in sequential budgeted first-price auctions; thus the problem remains open.

sources.title: Learning in Repeated Auctions with Budgets: Regret Minimization and Equilibrium
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces pacing/shading-style learning guarantees for budget-constrained bidders in repeated auctions. Proves in adversarial environments a bidder can guarantee competitive ratio \(\max\{1,T/B\}\) against a strong benchmark (best bid sequence), and in stochastic i.i.d. settings achieves near-optimality (competitive ratio 1 with \(O(\sqrt{T})\) regret) under suitable conditions.
sources.connection: Provides the canonical learning benchmark and techniques for budgeted repeated auctions; the open problem asks whether strengthening the benchmark class (e.g., Lipschitz bidding functions) or moving to stochastic values yields strictly better liquid-welfare guarantees than fixed shading multipliers.

sources.title: Budget Pacing in Repeated Auctions: Regret and Efficiency without Convergence
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows that under i.i.d. values and when bidders use a specific pacing algorithm, liquid welfare in repeated first- and second-price auctions is within factor 2 of optimal up to sublinear additive error. Also proves impossibility in sequential second-price auctions: even with no-regret, liquid welfare can be arbitrarily bad.
sources.connection: Establishes the baseline 2-approximation in a stochastic model but under algorithm-specific assumptions, and highlights that first-price is the promising format. The open problem asks whether stronger learning guarantees (e.g., richer benchmark classes) can beat the fixed-multiplier-based bounds in sequential first-price auctions.

sources.title: Autobidders with Budget and ROI Constraints: Efficiency, Regret, and Pacing Dynamics
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops adaptive pacing dynamics for autobidders with budget and ROI constraints, giving no-regret-type guarantees and showing resulting outcomes achieve a factor-2 liquid-welfare approximation under their model assumptions.
sources.connection: Represents progress on connecting learning dynamics to liquid-welfare efficiency beyond fixed multipliers, but still largely within pacing-style strategy classes; informs whether richer benchmark classes might improve worst-case liquid welfare.

sources.title: Online Learning with Knapsacks: the Best of Both Worlds
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides bandits-with-knapsacks algorithms that achieve near-optimal performance in stochastic settings (competitive ratio 1) while retaining robust guarantees in adversarial settings (competitive ratio scaling like \(T/B\)), with high-probability bounds.
sources.connection: The original paper reduces budgeted first-price learning to adversarial BwK; improvements in BwK (especially for structured action spaces) are a key tool if one wants to compete with richer benchmark classes such as Lipschitz bidding functions.

sources.title: Approximately Stationary Bandits with Knapsacks
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies BwK in intermediate regimes between stochastic and adversarial, giving algorithms with guarantees that interpolate between competitive ratio 1 (stochastic) and \(T/B\) (adversarial), with high-probability bounds.
sources.connection: Directly used by the source paper to obtain high-probability competitive ratio guarantees. Such intermediate-regime results are relevant to the open problem’s stochastic-values variant and may be extendable to richer benchmark classes.

sources.title: Bandits with Knapsacks
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the BwK framework and gives algorithms achieving near-optimal regret/competitive performance in the stochastic setting (competitive ratio 1 up to sublinear terms), forming the basis for later adversarial and best-of-both-worlds results.
sources.connection: If values are i.i.d. (stochastic model), BwK-style results suggest stronger per-bidder learning guarantees may be achievable, potentially enabling improved liquid-welfare bounds compared to adversarial settings.

sources.title: Adversarial Bandits with Knapsacks
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops adversarial BwK algorithms with polylogarithmic competitive ratios and regret bounds, and establishes lower bounds showing limitations when budgets are small (e.g., \(B\ll \sqrt{T}\)).
sources.connection: Provides the adversarial-learning backbone for budgeted sequential auctions; limitations here may translate into limits on what richer benchmark classes can guarantee in worst-case value sequences.

sources.title: Contextual Standard Auctions with Budgets: Revenue Equivalence and Efficiency Guarantees
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes budget management via value shading in (contextual) first-price auctions under distributional assumptions, establishing equilibrium and efficiency properties (including constant-factor welfare-type guarantees) in those models.
sources.connection: Represents the distributional/auction-theoretic side of shading-based bidding in first-price auctions. The open problem’s stochastic-values direction aligns with this line, and suggests that richer bidding rules might be analyzable under i.i.d. contexts.
